result:
There’s an old café on the edge of the Zattere where the light turns the color of apricots at exactly four-thirty in the afternoon, a phenomenon I first noticed twenty-three years ago when I was barely old enough to drink espresso without wincing and still young enough to believe that verbal photographs—sentences that try to approximate a moment exactly as the eye receives it—were more honest than narrative. I was wrong, but we can come back to that. The café has changed hands twice since then. First it belonged to a man named Franco whose left arm was two inches shorter than his right and who claimed to have taught himself English by reading Sylvia Plath with a flashlight on the Lido dunes, an implausible story that nevertheless felt true because his tattoos matched the fonts in my American edition of Ariel. The second owner, Giustina, sold it at a loss after her father developed the same tremor in his jaw that I had watched devour my own grandfather. Now it’s really a holding property for a multinational chain that puts macarons in plastic domes because gelato liquifies too quickly under LED spots, but the terrazzo floor is still the same, which means I keep returning and pretending nothing has changed.

I do this everywhere: find the one unmoved thing—sometimes it’s only a nail hole in a wall, sometimes an entire island—and double my life against it. It isn’t nostalgia, or at least not the retrospective, bullet-point nostalgia we weaponize to sand down the edges of whatever wounded us. More like a spinal reflex. The way sea birds roll one eye at the horizon to fix a point so they don’t lose the shoreline. Because I have moved too much, because most front doors I have walked through no longer open onto anything the guidebooks mention, because I learned the grammar of leaving before I learned how to conjugate the verb to stay, I need ballast. A child who rearranges entire rooms at night needs one picture frame to stay crooked so the world is still recognizable in the morning.  

The apricot light, then. It happens because the Giudecca canal is angled in such a way that, from mid-April to late October, its surface acts as a lagoon-wide reflector, and at four-thirty the sun’s ray hits a strip of copper roofing on the Redentore and throws a bath of warm sediment back toward the Zattere from almost exactly due south. By five the light has aged into persimmon; by six it’s burnt orange. I chart these gradations like ship logs. Which isn’t as eccentric as it sounds; before satellite navigation sailors measured longitude by how the color of water shifted from cobalt to ultramarine. Everything stalls for seven minutes around the apricot point. Tourists lower their phones, and even the Venetian boys who treat cappuccino like an afterthought hold still among verbs that normally punch holes in afternoons. Nothing numinous, just a collective pause in the city-wide exhale.

When I moved back to Venice last spring after almost a decade of mailed forwarding addresses (Berlin, Tokyo, a muddy island in British Columbia where the postman arrived by kayak), I re-instated the ritual. Every day I bring a notebook I pretend is new and sit at the same corner table where the scar from a now-vanished crab-grater jukebox interrupts the marble. I write what might still be called sentences without caring whether they connect. Word heaps. Arrangements that resemble musical scaffolding more than stories. My friend from graduate school, Lotte, writes entire novels this way and then, three years later, gives them plots. I lack her discipline, but I appreciate the aspirational posture.  

Last Thursday, the wind shifted and the apricot flash arrived at 4:42 instead of 4:30, a delay so insolent I tasted metal in my molars. I looked up to tell the barista—Paola from Mestre, whose favorite film is Attack of the 50 Foot Woman—only to discover she had been replaced by a boy with a Surrey accent and a single dimple that made him appear perennially undecided. The chain apparently runs its staff through rotational modules like a naval academy. I left without ordering and walked south until the fondamenta ended in a construction gate. Near the waterline a splintered gondola stern—black paint peeling like burnt toast—lay upside down like a beetle. Someone had scrawled across the hull i morti non pagano l’affitto, the dead don’t pay rent. Which is both true and the sharpest, most Venetian epitaph imaginable.

That night I dreamed my mother was rowing this broken gondola down the central aisle of the Minneapolis library where I first learned what an aisle was. The boat shrank as she propelled it; by the time she reached World Languages it was the size of a saltshaker. She addressed the tiny vessel as though it were me: “Why did we organize music by含水率?” The words didn’t fit; the consonants collided and struck sparks. When I woke, the humidity in my room was incalculable because the meter on the wall had stopped at 84 and the humidity itself kept going even beyond reason. I opened the shutters and water poured in from both directions, air-water and canal-water, and the distinction no longer seemed urgent.  

Venice teaches you—not intellectually, but skin-first—how permeable the borders are between categories. Water and stone wear each other’s names. A calle becomes a rio when the tide surges; conversely, when the city auditions to become Atlantis its campi sink and become simplified drawings of themselves. Ecosystem pride swaps wardrobes with urban decay. One morning you notice the palazzo opposite your window has become a maritime reef and you feel less like a resident than an unreliable crustacean taking notes.  

The first owner, Franco, liked to argue that weather in Venice was an intertext. In high summer, heat emerged from marble like the final sentences of a paragraph I hadn’t realized I was reading. Winter acqua alta was a footnote that grew into marginalia until the entire margin swallowed the text. When I replay his voice now, I understand he wasn’t being romantic; he was simply bored. When every day is postcard-perfect, nuance migrates to aberration: a 3-degree drop registers as epic. Which may explain why Venetians gossip so prodigiously—they are literary critics picking at the microclimate novel.  

Returning here has made me newly disciplined about watching strangers because every face might be masking the previous owner of my favorite bench. On Tuesday a woman sat in Franco’s old barber chair (it survives as an ironic furnish of the chain) reading Buzzati’s The Tartar Steppe. She underlined nothing, but her lips moved in the subjunctive. We exchanged five glances and two nods; the sixth glance would have committed us to conversation, so we both chickened out. Still, I recognized her posture—feet hooked at the ankles, right shoulder tucked forward—from the way my sixth-grade teacher read to us after recess. Sometimes we magnetize past templates onto present objects the way barnacles colonize hulls.

Overkill simile? Possibly. But staying guarded about comparisons is how cities become theme parks. By which I mean: there are two kinds of civic death. One is when nothing changes; the other is when nothing is allowed to change except at the rate of credit-card meters. Venice has been predicting its own demise since 1173, a longevity almost no longer fashionable. Its kitsch is its desperate heartbeat. Even pigeons here work overtime to look like silhouettes from 1950s snow globes. Yet a city that fears its own shadow and simultaneously sells the shadow in key-chains at two for €5 has earned the right to absurdity.

Last month a cruise ship sideswiped the Giudecca embankment and clipped the vaporetto stop my mother used when she visited in 1995. The newspaper quoted a Czech tourist who described the sound like “metal chewing bone.” The result was three surgically severed fingers and an acceleration in the conversation—ongoing since doges wore pantaloons—about whether Venice should still allow cruise ships. (Pronounce “still allow” so the stress falls on still, centuries flexing behind the syllable.) My favorite part was the photograph of the ship taken from above: a shoebox the size of four city blocks patting the canal on the head the way a careless uncle tousles a child’s hair too hard. The bruise stayed in the water for hours. I walked past the site later that evening. No one mentioned it. We Venice-adjacent live daily with versions of the beautiful tyrant: incomparable art and infrastructural cardiac arrest.

 Childhood is where this taught resignation starts. Imagine being eight. Imagine sitting on the windowsill of a third-floor campo-facing apartment, doing math homework, while outside the pavement raises itself like bread dough because the acqua alta is coming. Your mother stuffs shoes into plastic bags; your father runs downstairs to unchain the metal barriers; downstairs Mario from the bakery kisses a crucifix and three umbrellas at once. Water seeps over the sill; your worksheet dissolves. You remain fascinated that paper disintegrates so politely. The mark you left is not trauma but taxonomy: you learn to file experiences by opacity. Mathematics: transparent, gone. Kisses inside plastic boots: opaque, permanent.

Years later you revisit the same campo at low tide, operating under the adult superstition that if you stand at the exact coordinates you might unwrite the memory and leave it unstressed for the next eight-year-old. You wait; the stones are sun-stunned and obstinate. A German tourist asks if the “water show” is daily. You answer in Italian because you resent the word show. You do not correct him; your own grasp of Venetian dialect is as shaky as a rented GPS. By now you’ve understood symbiosis: you need tourists the way a carousel needs crank-handlers, even as they erase your childhood terrazzo with wheeled luggage measuring time in souvenir inertia.  

 There are micro-loyalties that salvage the day. In the morning I buy bread at a bakery I’ve sworn by since Franco was still capable of intact syllables. The baker is short and taciturn; he uses seawater yeast collected at Chioggia and refuses to sell croissants on principle. His sourdough reminds me of woodsmoke and public libraries. One slice and I can inhabit the body I occupied at eleven, biting through crust while Maura next door explained what a mosquito’s proboscis looked like using an eyebrow pencil and a napkin. The brain stores memories by synesthetic overlap: taste opens a tunnel to sound, to scent, to whatever story you assigned to the sound. The city is a cabinet of such tunnels. The danger is navigating them without losing the purchase on the present; once you open too many you live like a collapsed star, dense but all in the past.

When Lotte wrote her novel about Kraków, she embedded the scent of kerosene in the opening paragraph and then withheld any mention of it until page 237—not an easy task in a 245-page book. She argued that desire works like that, re-erupts when you’ve forgotten the geography of your own wanting. Venice smells of kerosene at dawn in winter when the garbage boats fire their engines near the Mercato. I always think of Lotte when the first bruised blast travels across San Polo. She lives now in Montreal and emails me photographs of snow that looks like punctuation: commas, full stops, the occasional ellipsis.

In March the city flooded again, fifteen centimeters higher than last year, which was already higher than any since 1966. I joined the brigades who carried café chairs above their heads in waist-deep water so shopkeepers could reopen before noon. A girl from Sicily invented a bucket line that resembled children’s choreography. In the afternoon, I borrowed rubber boots from someone named Massimo and walked to San Sebastiano where Veronese’s ceiling frescoes floated above puddles as if they knew their watery clones below were merely rehearsal. I knelt and saw my booted reflection clasping hands with a cherub. Suspension: art mimicking liquid scarcity; liquid mocking art’s insistence on permanence. I laughed so hard a security guard came over to check my pupils. He allowed me to stay because, as he put it, “tears look enough like high spirits that maybe they’re healthy.”  

I remember being ashamed of crying before age fifteen; now I just aim for viscosity regulation. In Venice there’s no shame in emotional overflow because the entire city leaks regularly. Underneath each campo lies a cistern of five million liters of harvested rainwater; when you tread on the paving stones you travel across an archival sponge. Every so often archaeologists core it and find leather soles from 1568, onion skins, a child’s marble, toy vessels no larger than a thimble. dredges. Poetry in negative space. The child’s marble surprised me the first time I read the report: it was germanic blue glass containing a swirl of white that looked like a miniature cyclone. Imagine that marble deep inside the earth and still spinning.

My father sent a photograph yesterday of my childhood home in St. Paul at sunset. The elm out front has been replaced by an ash, which will soon be replaced by nothing, since emerald borer larvae are devouring the Midwest. He insists the light is still the same, the unimpeachable gold of late September, god’s own highlighter. I messaged back: “The light is never the same.” He understood, perhaps, because he added three crying-laughing emojis, which is how we forgive each other across intergenerational translation.

Venice is adoptive rather than biological; I chose her the way a stray cat chooses the person least likely to keep fish in pockets. I arrived at nineteen with a Neapolitan phrasebook and a fear of water. My first night, I wandered into Santa Maria Gloriosa and found myself alone under Titian’s Assumption where Mary’s arms arc upward like bolide refuse and cherubs tumble sideways as if auditioning for meteor. The church smelled of candle smoke and wet plaster; I tasted incense like coins at the back of my tongue. That viscerality inoculated me against everything I later learned in art history seminars. Long before I understood iconography I knew my job was not to decipher but to witness.

Franco used to say that the greatest Venetians are almost horizontal. Titian was buried at the Frari at an angle; Vivaldi’s bones are rumored to crack jokes. Perspective, he claimed, is suspicious of the vertical. Even corpses know better. Walking the narrow calli teaches you empathic curvature: shoulders tilt, gait follows. You learn to navigate merciless corners by turning your torso instead of your ideas.

 Last week I met a poet from Bogotá wearing a trench coat lined with poplin pockets for different notebooks. We spoke first about veracity of syllables and then about daughters; hers lives in Australia, mine in Kyoto. The overlap was narrow but glowing. By sunset we were reciting Rilke in karaoke cadence while the Zattere apricot happened. At the exact moment what should have been the last line, a barge passed whose wake slapped the embankment soaking our cuffs. We laughed so hard the index cards I carried became illegible. Meaning: sometimes it is water that edits the text.

Language erosion keeps pace with physical. Venetian dialect—the variant spoken on Burano until the fifties—survives in snippets like postcards accidentally dropped inside books. I collect them obsessively. A grocer at Rialto sells me zucchini using “gòto” for glass instead of standard bicchiere; the syllable vibrates like marbles spun in a bowl. I repeat it to my reflection until my mouth remembers. Last month I found an eighty-year-old woman who remembers “cioèga,” meaning blind, still used as tender insult in her family. She repeated it twice, then broke into a cough; with her breath I smelled mint and history. These lexemes are edible: tiny sardine bones of phonemes hiding inside tissue.

I have been trying to write a speculative atlas for years. Not mythical, but surgical: each street corner annotated with the last word spoken there before the speaker walked away forever. The project stalls because you can’t interview absence. Yet sometimes absence interviews you. I was crossing Campo San Giacomo dell’Orio at 3 a.m. after a concert of whirled glasses—literal wineglasses spun on fingertips creating overtones—when a man shouted, in English unmistakly drunk, “If we forget to fall in love, the windows forget to breathe.” Then vanished down a calle so thin I still don’t believe it accommodated shoulders. I wrote the sentence down and cross-referenced with sunrise to see if the ferrules on the shutters looked any less oxygenated. Within a week the phrase had flipped into dialect: se dimenteghemmo ‘ndar in malora, i vetri desmente le rie. I mouth it now like a man counting rosaries invented last night.

Weather engines: the Adriatic barrels across the lagoon each winter with a snarl of low pressure that locals call sciente. To pronounce it you have to decide whether the c is soft or hard, then allow the whole word to hiss like sand against glass. Sciente snaps umbrellas backward; it teaches laundry lines the physics of antiparallel desire. During sciente dogs refuse to walk, even golden retrievers turned timid by 60-knot syllables. Humans talk louder to compensate, so winter bars become opera practice. When streets exhale water underfoot and wind from above it’s impossible to know which way to lean. The resultant choreography resembles hesitant courting; you bow to water, curtsy to wind, meanwhile your groceries rotate 90 degrees inside recyclable fiber bags.

I lost my first copy of Calvino’s Invisible Cities inside a pension’s radiator during sciente in 2007. The pages curled so loudly I thought they’d start a fire. When I returned the next day, the book was gone. The entire radiator had been replaced; in its place a sticker depicting St. Christopher smoothing a child’s furrowed forehead. I checked out another copy from the Biblioteca Marciana, already warped from damp. Marginalia announced itself: previous reader had penciled “each city is a detour from the city inside your mother” beside the discourse on Zaira. I never erased the note. We annotate each other without consent; it’s the kindest trespass.

For six months after returning this spring I kept waking at 4:26 exactly. Not 4:25, not 4:27. The digits felt like a lexicographer’s hallucination. I installed blackout curtains, replaced blue lights with red, tried dropping phone into rice bucket for chrissake airtight, nothing. One dawn I gave up and went for water-sound coffee instead. Campo Santa Margherita was empty except for a Brunello-hued cat twining through padlocks that some previous tenant had left on the iron rail, a ritual of affection misunderstood as security. The barista—a Romanian poet named Doru—makes espresso so thick you could stamp envelopes with it. At precisely 4:57, the cathedral bell tolled twice despite the official schedule listing no summons. Doru shrugged: “Mystery subscription.” I asked whether he ever wakes at symmetrical times; he flipped a demitasse and proceeded to recite from Mihai Eminescu translated into Venetian dialect. By 5:10 we agreed insomnia is just geographic dislocation refusing to admit it has arrived. I have never woken at 4:26 again.

Speaking of Eminescu: there’s a canal that bisects Dorsoduro called del Ognissanti. On May 9 each year leaflets from Latteria students paper the bridges describing how the poet arrived Venice days before his death in 1889 attempting to cure what doctors then called “metaphysical vertigo.” Whether factual or not is irrelevant; urban myth behaves like lagoon water: permeates, then leaves salt residue you can taste for weeks. The leaflets ask participants to leave bouquets of saxifrage on any windowsill above waterline. By midday the steps of San Sebastiano bloom white with tiny star-like flowers. I brought saxifrage last year and overheard an Australian backpacker explaining to her boyfriend, “These are for a vampire Romanian; apparently the flowers stop him from rising.” I didn’t correct her. In the afternoon the tide rose enough to sweep an armful into the canal where they floated like alphabet soup. I contemplated how grief relocates nationality.

 My childhood best friend, Amy, arrived last September. We hadn’t seen each other since college when she burned her acceptance letter to medical school and left town with a street magician. Now she teaches refugee children in Jordan how to build kites from confiscated propaganda flyers. She emailed: “I need a moon made of stone for forty-eight hours, then returning to sand.” Venice performs lunar gravity by accident. The salt air slows everyone’s syllables, the walls bleach to crater white. We met at 9 p.m. under the Rialto arches. She smelled of Istanbul bus exhaust and sesame. We bought sandwiches dense enough to stop clocks, walked until calle became campo became canal became camp again. She asked if I’d ever forgiven myself for leaving St. Paul. We spoke in questions: “Are we verbs yet?” “If silence is currency, who are we budgeting?” Near dawn we found a water taxi driver napping in his craft with feet propped on the throttle. She dropped a saxifrage inside his rolled newspaper. He woke smiling, interpreting the gesture as the city’s apology for overtime, which it was, in a way.

On marzipan Saturdays, Venice allows itself planetary quirks. One such Saturday last October, the moon did in fact appear corona-large because warm air trapped higher up diffracted its light into rainbows. I witnessed from the Accademia bridge where a Venezuelan cellist busked Bach suites via transducer implanted beneath the skin; sound emanated from her collarbone. I dropped a coin into her open violin case, but what she accepted instead was the receipt from my grocery run. She rolled it into rosettes and plucked them like pizzicato. At the precise moment the moon achieved its halo, her bow string broke; the sound resembled a stork landing. We laughed until tributary. Then I carried bags home through shutter-clamped sestiere where curtain seams exhaled cooking smells like care packages from ancestors. My apartment smelled faintly of cardamom and electricity. I boiled tea and listed unusual couplings I’d witnessed that month: a pigeon befriending a typographer, an octogenarian singing reggaeton backwards, a schoolboy sketching pigeons with mechanical pencils taped to reed pipes. All of which confirmed that the world rests inside continuous draft status; publish prematurely, revise with weather.

Economics: I survive off translation gigs, mainly Spanish into Italian subtitles for agro-docu series. My screen flickers with endless furrows, slow-motion grains suffocating in luxury close-ups. Rendering campesino idioms into Venetian requires linguistic origami. ‘Maíz criollo’ becomes ‘polenta antiga,’ though nothing suggests tangibility like expressive proximity. The pay is negligible, but it allows me to purchase glueless rice, espresso, memory sticks. One gig offered cryptocurrency which I declined citing the city’s salt corrosion; imagine wallets rusting. Instead I received free coffee vouchers from Doru who considers exposure fair trade. On really broke mornings, he waves me off with a preemptive gesture like I’m a kindly ghost who owes centuries rather than euros.

There’s a ferryman, Tancredi, older than muck, who once told me a story. As a boy during World War II he watched Germans use church bells as target practice. When liberation came, Venetians drowned the Nazi ammunition in the canals and left them there as low-door reefs. He claims the bells remained on seabed ringing at low tide, a rumor no diver confirms because sonar doesn’t speak cryptography. I asked whether the ringing keeps awake the disobedient ghosts. He replied: lions sleep standing, fishes obey melody, and stories serve whichever mouth is hungriest. Then he ferried me across Giudecca without charging because I reminded him supposedly of his cousin Lucia who defected to Australia with a mandolin in 1947. identity postcards shuffle like tarot.

Memory is a palace you can only enter when its owners are asleep. Thereforney, phantom keys. The trick is to leave before the lights come on. Last autumn I followed a cat so gray it appeared monochrome beneath sodium streetlight; she led me through a previously unnoticed Fondamenta ending in a brick wall covered in so many concert posters the paper layers resembled eczema. I peeled one: a 1996 performance by Emir Kusturica soundtracked by Giora Feidman. In pencil on reverse someone had written REVUOI, invented word meaning to recite a memory until it changes color. I pocketed it. Later my pocket filled with canal water during high tide; ink softened, letters bled into waveforms. When the paper dried it was taut as drumskin except where R had become an anchor and U a small bird. Artifacts continue revising even under archival arrest.

Talking about eleventh-hour revelations sticks me on the same sandbar where unfinished thoughts rot. But there’s another way, maybe. I have begun recording pedestrian opera: fragments of conversation, lullabies climbing from washing windows, the bronchial cough a chapel makes when doves leave abruptly. Once a week I hike to Forte Marghera with recorder and microphone shaped like a licorice stick. That land used to be postcards depicting Austrian soldiers amid linden trees; now it’s wild grass battling concrete shards. I collect sound bacteria. The other day I captured a teenager on the ferry humming a tune which turned out to be the barcode scanning sound from supermarket used in four-four time. When layered over church bells it creates accidental polyrhythm. Most evenings I store files in external drives labeled after chemical elements: Sodium for nostalgia, Argon for dialogue, Xenon for sirens at erroneous midnight. A periodic table of displaced acoustics.

Last Thursday an old woman named Gisella summoned me to read aloud from a letter she received in 1983 but only opened now. She feared its contents might combust if exposed to October oxygen. The letter was from her lover who drowned trying to prevent a migrant boat from capsizing near Lampedusa. It began, “Se quell’acqua avesse avuto delle labbra—If that water had lips.” I broke off after the first sentence and instead we sat silently listening to her Bosch radio hiss. Eventually she asked me to walk her to the canal where they first kissed. Midday prowlers required navigation; we made unspoken pact not to stop at windows displaying new shoes because Gisella’s balance was tethered more to memory than ground. At water’s edge we stood. She retied her scarf three times, then untethered the envelope and let the wind accept it. Pages floated like gulls initially, then absorbed, darkened, became unreadable punctuation. She whispered closing lines I could not catch. Afterwards we drank espresso at Dodo Caffè where she ordered decaf as theological compromise.

Tomorrow I will attend the traditional Festa della Sensa, an annual reenactment of Venice’s marriage to the sea. Every year the mayor throws a gold ring—newly purchased because the city doesn’t have archival funds for sentimentality—into the lagoon while intoning “We wed thee, oh sea.” On the surface it’s a touristic tambourine, but underneath, silence. The sea has never contested the vows. If she ever demanded divorce Via alimony, entire palazzi would migrate to Swiss banks. The ceremony is scheduled 11 a.m. sharp. In 2005 the ring fell short and-- rumor—a yacht captain dove and found it months later during hull cleaning; he sold it to pay graving dock repairs. My plan is to observe from San Giorgio and record the exact pitch of water receiving the metaphor. Spectacle is forgivable if rendered into data.

 And wherelarge strangers ask why I remain here—inflated rents, recurring flooding, crowds so thick you rehearse breathing from alternate centuries—I remember Gisella at canal edge. The way her hand trembled inside mine was revolutionary fire written small. Connection across latitudes and decades is maybe the only iceberg not yet melted. Each act of attention is vessel, seaworthy regardless of ballast.

In the end, I suppose “end” is the wrong verb. Regroup. In the regrouping, here’s what sprouts: apricot light at 4:30 again, arriving on schedule today as if to spoon-feed continuity. The boy Surrey accent attempted latte art shaped like gondolas; it collapsed mid-foam into abstract geometry. Meanwhile, a child outside traced infinity on the pavement using melted ice cream. She wore dinosaur socks and surgical mask patterned with tiny starfish. I gave her a saxifrage head; she popped it into her mouth like candy. Her mother smiled parental apology. I said it’s tradition, let her taste St. Mark’s bells. As they walked away she hummed transducer-bone, the same Bach Sarabande Lucia once sent across Australian coastlines. Nothing completes, everything corresponds. We ferry our incomplete sentences from century to century and sign the water’s surface with provisional declarations of persistence.

A calle becomes a rio when the tide surges; conversely, when the city auditions to become Atlantis its campi sink and become simplified drawings of themselves. Ecosystem pride swaps wardrobes with urban decay. One morning you notice the palazzo opposite your window has become a maritime reef and you feel less like a resident than an unreliable crustacean taking notes.  

The first owner, Franco, liked to argue that weather in Venice was an intertext. In high summer, heat emerged from marble like the final sentences of a paragraph I hadn’t realized I was reading. Winter acqua alta was a footnote that grew into marginalia until the entire margin swallowed the text. When I replay his voice now, I understand he wasn’t being romantic; he was simply bored. When every day is postcard-perfect, nuance migrates to aberration: a 3-degree drop registers as epic. Which may explain why Venetians gossip so prodigiously—they are literary critics picking at the microclimate novel.  

Returning here has made me newly disciplined about watching strangers because every face might be masking the previous owner of my favorite bench. On Tuesday a woman sat in Franco’s old barber chair (it survives as an ironic furnish of the chain) reading Buzzati’s The Tartar Steppe. She underlined nothing, but her lips moved in the subjunctive. We exchanged five glances and two nods; the sixth glance would have committed us to conversation, so we both chickened out. Still, I recognized her posture—feet hooked at the ankles, right shoulder tucked forward—from the way my sixth-grade teacher read to us after recess. Sometimes we magnetize past templates onto present objects the way barnacles colonize hulls.

Overkill simile? Possibly. But staying guarded about comparisons is how cities become theme parks. By which I mean: there are two kinds of civic death. One is when nothing changes; the other is when nothing is allowed to change except at the rate of credit-card meters. Venice has been predicting its own demise since 1173, a longevity almost no longer fashionable. Its kitsch is its desperate heartbeat. Even pigeons here work overtime to look like silhouettes from 1950s snow globes. Yet a city that fears its own shadow and simultaneously sells the shadow in key-chains at two for €5 has earned the right to absurdity.

Last month a cruise ship sideswiped the Giudecca embankment and clipped the vaporetto stop my mother used when she visited in 1995. The newspaper quoted a Czech tourist who described the sound like “metal chewing bone.” The result was three surgically severed fingers and an acceleration in the conversation—ongoing since doges wore pantaloons—about whether Venice should still allow cruise ships. (Pronounce “still allow” so the stress falls on still, centuries flexing behind the syllable.) My favorite part was the photograph of the ship taken from above: a shoebox the size of four city blocks patting the canal on the head the way a careless uncle tousles a child’s hair too hard. The bruise stayed in the water for hours. I walked past the site later that evening. No one mentioned it. We Venice-adjacent live daily with versions of the beautiful tyrant: incomparable art and infrastructural cardiac arrest.

 Childhood is where this taught resignation starts. Imagine being eight. Imagine sitting on the windowsill of a third-floor campo-facing apartment, doing math homework, while outside the pavement raises itself like bread dough because the acqua alta is coming. Your mother stuffs shoes into plastic bags; your father runs downstairs to unchain the metal barriers; downstairs Mario from the bakery kisses a crucifix and three umbrellas at once. Water seeps over the sill; your worksheet dissolves. You remain fascinated that paper disintegrates so politely. The mark you left is not trauma but taxonomy: you learn to file experiences by opacity. Mathematics: transparent, gone. Kisses inside plastic boots: opaque, permanent.

Years later you revisit the same campo at low tide, operating under the adult superstition that if you stand at the exact coordinates you might unwrite the memory and leave it unstressed for the next eight-year-old. You wait; the stones are sun-stunned and obstinate. A German tourist asks if the “water show” is daily. You answer in Italian because you resent the word show. You do not correct him; your own grasp of Venetian dialect is as shaky as a rented GPS. By now you’ve understood symbiosis: you need tourists the way a carousel needs crank-handlers, even as they erase your childhood terrazzo with wheeled luggage measuring time in souvenir inertia.  

 There are micro-loyalties that salvage the day. In the morning I buy bread at a bakery I’ve sworn by since Franco was still capable of intact syllables. The baker is short and taciturn; he uses seawater yeast collected at Chioggia and refuses to sell croissants on principle. His sourdough reminds me of woodsmoke and public libraries. One slice and I can inhabit the body I occupied at eleven, biting through crust while Maura next door explained what a mosquito’s proboscis looked like using an eyebrow pencil and a napkin. The brain stores memories by synesthetic overlap: taste opens a tunnel to sound, to scent, to whatever story you assigned to the sound. The city is a cabinet of such tunnels. The danger is navigating them without losing the purchase on the present; once you open too many you live like a collapsed star, dense but all in the past.

When Lotte wrote her novel about Kraków, she embedded the scent of kerosene in the opening paragraph and then withheld any mention of it until page 237—not an easy task in a 245-page book. She argued that desire works like that, re-erupts when you’ve forgotten the geography of your own wanting. Venice smells of kerosene at dawn in winter when the garbage boats fire their engines near the Mercato. I always think of Lotte when the first bruised blast travels across San Polo. She lives now in Montreal and emails me photographs of snow that looks like punctuation: commas, full stops, the occasional ellipsis.

In March the city flooded again, fifteen centimeters higher than last year, which was already higher than any since 1966. I joined the brigades who carried café chairs above their heads in waist-deep water so shopkeepers could reopen before noon. A girl from Sicily invented a bucket line that resembled children’s choreography. In the afternoon, I borrowed rubber boots from someone named Massimo and walked to San Sebastiano where Veronese’s ceiling frescoes floated above puddles as if they knew their watery clones below were merely rehearsal. I knelt and saw my booted reflection clasping hands with a cherub. Suspension: art mimicking liquid scarcity; liquid mocking art’s insistence on permanence. I laughed so hard a security guard came over to check my pupils. He allowed me to stay because, as he put it, “tears look enough like high spirits that maybe they’re healthy.”  

I remember being ashamed of crying before age fifteen; now I just aim for viscosity regulation. In Venice there’s no shame in emotional overflow because the entire city leaks regularly. Underneath each campo lies a cistern of five million liters of harvested rainwater; when you tread on the paving stones you travel across an archival sponge. Every so often archaeologists core it and find leather soles from 1568, onion skins, a child’s marble, toy vessels no larger than a thimble. dredges. Poetry in negative space. The child’s marble surprised me the first time I read the report: it was germanic blue glass containing a swirl of white that looked like a miniature cyclone. Imagine that marble deep inside the earth and still spinning.

My father sent a photograph yesterday of my childhood home in St. Paul at sunset. The elm out front has been replaced by an ash, which will soon be replaced by nothing, since emerald borer larvae are devouring the Midwest. He insists the light is still the same, the unimpeachable gold of late September, god’s own highlighter. I messaged back: “The light is never the same.” He understood, perhaps, because he added three crying-laughing emojis, which is how we forgive each other across intergenerational translation.

Venice is adoptive rather than biological; I chose her the way a stray cat chooses the person least likely to keep fish in pockets. I arrived at nineteen with a Neapolitan phrasebook and a fear of water. My first night, I wandered into Santa Maria Gloriosa and found myself alone under Titian’s Assumption where Mary’s arms arc upward like bolide refuse and cherubs tumble sideways as if auditioning for meteor. The church smelled of candle smoke and wet plaster; I tasted incense like coins at the back of my tongue. That viscerality inoculated me against everything I later learned in art history seminars. Long before I understood iconography I knew my job was not to decipher but to witness.

Franco used to say that the greatest Venetians are almost horizontal. Titian was buried at the Frari at an angle; Vivaldi’s bones are rumored to crack jokes. Perspective, he claimed, is suspicious of the vertical. Even corpses know better. Walking the narrow calli teaches you empathic curvature: shoulders tilt, gait follows. You learn to navigate merciless corners by turning your torso instead of your ideas.

 Last week I met a poet from Bogotá wearing a trench coat lined with poplin pockets for different notebooks. We spoke first about veracity of syllables and then about daughters; hers lives in Australia, mine in Kyoto. The overlap was narrow but glowing. By sunset we were reciting Rilke in karaoke cadence while the Zattere apricot happened. At the exact moment what should have been the last line, a barge passed whose wake slapped the embankment soaking our cuffs. We laughed so hard the index cards I carried became illegible. Meaning: sometimes it is water that edits the text.

Language erosion keeps pace with physical. Venetian dialect—the variant spoken on Burano until the fifties—survives in snippets like postcards accidentally dropped inside books. I collect them obsessively. A grocer at Rialto sells me zucchini using “gòto” for glass instead of standard bicchiere; the syllable vibrates like marbles spun in a bowl. I repeat it to my reflection until my mouth remembers. Last month I found an eighty-year-old woman who remembers “cioèga,” meaning blind, still used as tender insult in her family. She repeated it twice, then broke into a cough; with her breath I smelled mint and history. These lexemes are edible: tiny sardine bones of phonemes hiding inside tissue.

I have been trying to write a speculative atlas for years. Not mythical, but surgical: each street corner annotated with the last word spoken there before the speaker walked away forever. The project stalls because you can’t interview absence. Yet sometimes absence interviews you. I was crossing Campo San Giacomo dell’Orio at 3 a.m. after a concert of whirled glasses—literal wineglasses spun on fingertips creating overtones—when a man shouted, in English unmistakly drunk, “If we forget to fall in love, the windows forget to breathe.” Then vanished down a calle so thin I still don’t believe it accommodated shoulders. I wrote the sentence down and cross-referenced with sunrise to see if the ferrules on the shutters looked any less oxygenated. Within a week the phrase had flipped into dialect: se dimenteghemmo ‘ndar in malora, i vetri desmente le rie. I mouth it now like a man counting rosaries invented last night.

Weather engines: the Adriatic barrels across the lagoon each winter with a snarl of low pressure that locals call sciente. To pronounce it you have to decide whether the c is soft or hard, then allow the whole word to hiss like sand against glass. Sciente snaps umbrellas backward; it teaches laundry lines the physics of antiparallel desire. During sciente dogs refuse to walk, even golden retrievers turned timid by 60-knot syllables. Humans talk louder to compensate, so winter bars become opera practice. When streets exhale water underfoot and wind from above it’s impossible to know which way to lean. The resultant choreography resembles hesitant courting; you bow to water, curtsy to wind, meanwhile your groceries rotate 90 degrees inside recyclable fiber bags.

I lost my first copy of Calvino’s Invisible Cities inside a pension’s radiator during sciente in 2007. The pages curled so loudly I thought they’d start a fire. When I returned the next day, the book was gone. The entire radiator had been replaced; in its place a sticker depicting St. Christopher smoothing a child’s furrowed forehead. I checked out another copy from the Biblioteca Marciana, already warped from damp. Marginalia announced itself: previous reader had penciled “each city is a detour from the city inside your mother” beside the discourse on Zaira. I never erased the note. We annotate each other without consent; it’s the kindest trespass.

For six months after returning this spring I kept waking at 4:26 exactly. Not 4:25, not 4:27. The digits felt like a lexicographer’s hallucination. I installed blackout curtains, replaced blue lights with red, tried dropping phone into rice bucket for chrissake airtight, nothing. One dawn I gave up and went for water-sound coffee instead. Campo Santa Margherita was empty except for a Brunello-hued cat twining through padlocks that some previous tenant had left on the iron rail, a ritual of affection misunderstood as security. The barista—a Romanian poet named Doru—makes espresso so thick you could stamp envelopes with it. At precisely 4:57, the cathedral bell tolled twice despite the official schedule listing no summons. Doru shrugged: “Mystery subscription.” I asked whether he ever wakes at symmetrical times; he flipped a demitasse and proceeded to recite from Mihai Eminescu translated into Venetian dialect. By 5:10 we agreed insomnia is just geographic dislocation refusing to admit it has arrived. I have never woken at 4:26 again.

Speaking of Eminescu: there’s a canal that bisects Dorsoduro called del Ognissanti. On May 9 each year leaflets from Latteria students paper the bridges describing how the poet arrived Venice days before his death in 1889 attempting to cure what doctors then called “metaphysical vertigo.” Whether factual or not is irrelevant; urban myth behaves like lagoon water: permeates, then leaves salt residue you can taste for weeks. The leaflets ask participants to leave bouquets of saxifrage on any windowsill above waterline. By midday the steps of San Sebastiano bloom white with tiny star-like flowers. I brought saxifrage last year and overheard an Australian backpacker explaining to her boyfriend, “These are for a vampire Romanian; apparently the flowers stop him from rising.” I didn’t correct her. In the afternoon the tide rose enough to sweep an armful into the canal where they floated like alphabet soup. I contemplated how grief relocates nationality.

 My childhood best friend, Amy, arrived last September. We hadn’t seen each other since college when she burned her acceptance letter to medical school and left town with a street magician. Now she teaches refugee children in Jordan how to build kites from confiscated propaganda flyers. She emailed: “I need a moon made of stone for forty-eight hours, then returning to sand.” Venice performs lunar gravity by accident. The salt air slows everyone’s syllables, the walls bleach to crater white. We met at 9 p.m. under the Rialto arches. She smelled of Istanbul bus exhaust and sesame. We bought sandwiches dense enough to stop clocks, walked until calle became campo became canal became camp again. She asked if I’d ever forgiven myself for leaving St. Paul. We spoke in questions: “Are we verbs yet?” “If silence is currency, who are we budgeting?” Near dawn we found a water taxi driver napping in his craft with feet propped on the throttle. She dropped a saxifrage inside his rolled newspaper. He woke smiling, interpreting the gesture as the city’s apology for overtime, which it was, in a way.

On marzipan Saturdays, Venice allows itself planetary quirks. One such Saturday last October, the moon did in fact appear corona-large because warm air trapped higher up diffracted its light into rainbows. I witnessed from the Accademia bridge where a Venezuelan cellist busked Bach suites via transducer implanted beneath the skin; sound emanated from her collarbone. I dropped a coin into her open violin case, but what she accepted instead was the receipt from my grocery run. She rolled it into rosettes and plucked them like pizzicato. At the precise moment the moon achieved its halo, her bow string broke; the sound resembled a stork landing. We laughed until tributary. Then I carried bags home through shutter-clamped sestiere where curtain seams exhaled cooking smells like care packages from ancestors. My apartment smelled faintly of cardamom and electricity. I boiled tea and listed unusual couplings I’d witnessed that month: a pigeon befriending a typographer, an octogenarian singing reggaeton backwards, a schoolboy sketching pigeons with mechanical pencils taped to reed pipes. All of which confirmed that the world rests inside continuous draft status; publish prematurely, revise with weather.

Economics: I survive off translation gigs, mainly Spanish into Italian subtitles for agro-docu series. My screen flickers with endless furrows, slow-motion grains suffocating in luxury close-ups. Rendering campesino idioms into Venetian requires linguistic origami. ‘Maíz criollo’ becomes ‘polenta antiga,’ though nothing suggests tangibility like expressive proximity. The pay is negligible, but it allows me to purchase glueless rice, espresso, memory sticks. One gig offered cryptocurrency which I declined citing the city’s salt corrosion; imagine wallets rusting. Instead I received free coffee vouchers from Doru who considers exposure fair trade. On really broke mornings, he waves me off with a preemptive gesture like I’m a kindly ghost who owes centuries rather than euros.

There’s a ferryman, Tancredi, older than muck, who once told me a story. As a boy during World War II he watched Germans use church bells as target practice. When liberation came, Venetians drowned the Nazi ammunition in the canals and left them there as low-door reefs. He claims the bells remained on seabed ringing at low tide, a rumor no diver confirms because sonar doesn’t speak cryptography. I asked whether the ringing keeps awake the disobedient ghosts. He replied: lions sleep standing, fishes obey melody, and stories serve whichever mouth is hungriest. Then he ferried me across Giudecca without charging because I reminded him supposedly of his cousin Lucia who defected to Australia with a mandolin in 1947. identity postcards shuffle like tarot.

Memory is a palace you can only enter when its owners are asleep. Thereforney, phantom keys. The trick is to leave before the lights come on. Last autumn I followed a cat so gray it appeared monochrome beneath sodium streetlight; she led me through a previously unnoticed Fondamenta ending in a brick wall covered in so many concert posters the paper layers resembled eczema. I peeled one: a 1996 performance by Emir Kusturica soundtracked by Giora Feidman. In pencil on reverse someone had written REVUOI, invented word meaning to recite a memory until it changes color. I pocketed it. Later my pocket filled with canal water during high tide; ink softened, letters bled into waveforms. When the paper dried it was taut as drumskin except where R had become an anchor and U a small bird. Artifacts continue revising even under archival arrest.

Talking about eleventh-hour revelations sticks me on the same sandbar where unfinished thoughts rot. But there’s another way, maybe. I have begun recording pedestrian opera: fragments of conversation, lullabies climbing from washing windows, the bronchial cough a chapel makes when doves leave abruptly. Once a week I hike to Forte Marghera with recorder and microphone shaped like a licorice stick. That land used to be postcards depicting Austrian soldiers amid linden trees; now it’s wild grass battling concrete shards. I collect sound bacteria. The other day I captured a teenager on the ferry humming a tune which turned out to be the barcode scanning sound from supermarket used in four-four time. When layered over church bells it creates accidental polyrhythm. Most evenings I store files in external drives labeled after chemical elements: Sodium for nostalgia, Argon for dialogue, Xenon for sirens at erroneous midnight. A periodic table of displaced acoustics.

Last Thursday an old woman named Gisella summoned me to read aloud from a letter she received in 1983 but only opened now. She feared its contents might combust if exposed to October oxygen. The letter was from her lover who drowned trying to prevent a migrant boat from capsizing near Lampedusa. It began, “Se quell’acqua avesse avuto delle labbra—If that water had lips.” I broke off after the first sentence and instead we sat silently listening to her Bosch radio hiss. Eventually she asked me to walk her to the canal where they first kissed. Midday prowlers required navigation; we made unspoken pact not to stop at windows displaying new shoes because Gisella’s balance was tethered more to memory than ground. At water’s edge we stood. She retied her scarf three times, then untethered the envelope and let the wind accept it. Pages floated like gulls initially, then absorbed, darkened, became unreadable punctuation. She whispered closing lines I could not catch. Afterwards we drank espresso at Dodo Caffè where she ordered decaf as theological compromise.

Tomorrow I will attend the traditional Festa della Sensa, an annual reenactment of Venice’s marriage to the sea. Every year the mayor throws a gold ring—newly purchased because the city doesn’t have archival funds for sentimentality—into the lagoon while intoning “We wed thee, oh sea.” On the surface it’s a touristic tambourine, but underneath, silence. The sea has never contested the vows. If she ever demanded divorce Via alimony, entire palazzi would migrate to Swiss banks. The ceremony is scheduled 11 a.m. sharp. In 2005 the ring fell short and-- rumor—a yacht captain dove and found it months later during hull cleaning; he sold it to pay graving dock repairs. My plan is to observe from San Giorgio and record the exact pitch of water receiving the metaphor. Spectacle is forgivable if rendered into data.

 And wherelarge strangers ask why I remain here—inflated rents, recurring flooding, crowds so thick you rehearse breathing from alternate centuries—I remember Gisella at canal edge. The way her hand trembled inside mine was revolutionary fire written small. Connection across latitudes and decades is maybe the only iceberg not yet melted. Each act of attention is vessel, seaworthy regardless of ballast.

In the end, I suppose “end” is the wrong verb. Regroup. In the regrouping, here’s what sprouts: apricot light at 4:30 again, arriving on schedule today as if to spoon-feed continuity. The boy Surrey accent attempted latte art shaped like gondolas; it collapsed mid-foam into abstract geometry. Meanwhile, a child outside traced infinity on the pavement using melted ice cream. She wore dinosaur socks and surgical mask patterned with tiny starfish. I gave her a saxifrage head; she popped it into her mouth like candy. Her mother smiled parental apology. I said it’s tradition, let her taste St. Mark’s bells. As they walked away she hummed transducer-bone, the same Bach Sarabande Lucia once sent across Australian coastlines. Nothing completes, everything corresponds. We ferry our incomplete sentences from century to century and sign the water’s surface with provisional declarations of persistence.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
